comments.title: LPIPS interpretation as a diversity metric may mislead
comments.quote: IC-GAN exhibits notably higher LPIPS than LostGANv2 and OC-GAN, which could be explained by the fact that the latter only leverage one real sample per input conditioning during training; whereas IC-GAN uses multiple real neighboring samples per each instance, naturally favouring diversity in the generated images.
comments.explanation: At first glance, claiming that 'higher LPIPS' is desirable immediately struck me as contradictory, since LPIPS (Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity) is conventionally treated as a distance metric where lower values indicate better alignment or quality. I verified how the metric is used in the evaluation protocol (Section 3.1), which notes that LPIPS computes distances between activations of two images generated with different latents but the same conditioning. In GAN literature, computing LPIPS across a set of generated samples to measure pairwise dissimilarity is a valid way to quantify diversity, where higher scores indeed indicate more diverse outputs. However, because this directionality flips the standard 'lower is better' heuristic, a careful reader unfamiliar with this specific evaluation convention could reasonably misinterpret the result as degraded image quality or distribution mismatch. The authors' intent is correct, but the phrasing lacks the explicit caveat that LPIPS here measures intra-set perceptual dissimilarity, which could mislead a broader audience.

comments.title: Inconsistent recommendation of neighborhood size k across datasets
comments.quote: Overall, k = 50 offers a good compromise, exhibiting top performance across all metrics when using at least 500 stored instances.
comments.explanation: I first checked the experimental setup in Section 3.1, where the authors state they set k = 50 for ImageNet but explicitly chose k = 5 for both COCO-Stuff and ImageNet-LT. Later, in the neighborhood size analysis (Section 3.5), they conclude that k = 50 offers a good compromise and exhibits top performance. This creates a slight tension: if k = 50 is generally superior, why was k = 5 selected for the other datasets in the main experiments? I considered whether dataset-specific manifold densities or computational constraints might justify the lower k, but the text does not provide this rationale. The omission leaves the hyperparameter tuning strategy partially underspecified and may confuse readers attempting to transfer the method to new domains, as the guideline conflicts with the reported dataset-specific settings.

comments.title: Unaddressed implication of uniform weights in overlapping neighborhoods
comments.quote: Our objective is to model the underlying data distribution p( x ) as a mixture of conditional distributions p( x | h i ) around each of M instance feature vectors h i in the dataset, such that p( x ) ≈ 1/M ∑_i p( x | h i ).
comments.explanation: The authors approximate the global density as a uniform mixture over M instance conditionals, explicitly noting that the neighborhoods are overlapping. I initially wondered whether overlapping partitions would cause certain regions of the manifold to be over-represented (since they belong to multiple neighborhoods) while sparse regions are under-represented. In classical KDE, overlap is naturally handled by kernel bandwidths and often weighted by distance or density estimates. The authors do not acknowledge or justify why uniform mixing weights are sufficient despite the explicit overlap. While this is a common and practical simplification, omitting the assumption means the theoretical grounding is slightly loose: the approximation error heavily depends on neighborhood uniformity, which is dataset-dependent. For a method positioning itself as a non-parametric density estimator, a brief note on how uniform weights interact with overlap would strengthen the justification.

comments.title: Contradiction between neighborhood size $k$ and its claimed effect on generation diversity
comments.quote:
Summary: "Neighborhood size $k$ acts as a KDE-like bandwidth parameter: smaller $k$ enhances generation diversity but requires more stored instances, while larger $k$ yields smoother conditional distributions needing fewer instances."

Section 3.3: "Transferring the model from ImageNet boosts diversity w.r.t. the model trained on COCO-Stuff (see LPIPS in Table 2), which may be in part due to the larger $k=50$ used for ImageNet training, compared to $k=5$ when training on COCO-Stuff."
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by two statements that appear to assign opposite effects to the neighborhood size $k$ on generation diversity. In the summary, the authors explicitly state that 'smaller $k$ enhances generation diversity,' drawing an analogy to KDE bandwidth where a tighter neighborhood captures sharper, more localized modes. However, in Section 3.3, they attribute increased diversity (quantified via higher LPIPS, which measures perceptual distance between samples conditioned on the same instance) to the *larger* $k=50$ used during ImageNet training, contrasted with $k=5$ for COCO-Stuff. I checked the LPIPS definition in Section 3.1 to confirm it measures intra-conditioning variation, validating that higher LPIPS indeed corresponds to greater diversity. I also considered whether 'diversity' might be used ambiguously to mean dataset coverage/mode coverage in the summary versus within-condition variation in Section 3.3, but the direct causal phrasing ('due to the larger $k=50$...') creates a logical conflict. It remains unclear whether smaller neighborhoods increase diversity by forcing the mixture to cover more distinct modes across the dataset, or whether larger neighborhoods improve diversity by providing the discriminator with a richer, more varied set of real samples during adversarial training. The authors should clarify the mechanism and reconcile these statements.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper presents IC-GAN, an innovative method that integrates kernel density estimation (KDE) concepts into the GAN framework to address the challenges of modeling complex, high-dimensional distributions, particularly in unconditional settings. The manuscript is well-written and clearly articulates the motivation, effectively drawing parallels to KDE while distinguishing the proposed adversarial mechanism where the "kernel" is learned and conditioned on instance representations. The core contribution—partitioning the manifold into overlapping neighborhoods and using nearest neighbors as real samples for the discriminator—offers a theoretically sound approach to mitigating mode collapse and improving local density estimation. The demonstrated ability to transfer to unseen datasets and achieve competitive results in class-conditional settings further strengthens the paper's impact. However, the most significant issue is **computational scalability**: the reliance on nearest-neighbor lookups and neighbor sampling may introduce prohibitive overhead for large-scale datasets like ImageNet, a concern that must be rigorously quantified. Additionally, the **specific architectural mechanism for instance conditioning** requires clearer specification, as the efficiency and fidelity of generation heavily depend on how instance representations are integrated into the generator and discriminator. Finally, while the abstract claims improvements in diversity and realism, the evaluation would benefit from more granular metrics (e.g., precision/recall or coverage) to substantiate claims regarding mode collapse reduction and to provide a robust comparison against modern conditional baselines in the absence of labels.

comments.title: Fragmented opening sentence in Section 2
comments.quote: overlapping clusters in the data manifold, where each cluster is described by a datapoint **x** _i_ – referred to as _instance_ – and its nearest neighbors set _Ai_ in a feature space.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly establish the geometric intuition of modeling data via local neighborhoods. However, the opening sentence of Section 2 lacks a main clause or subject, beginning abruptly with a prepositional/participial phrase. It is unclear whether they intend to state 'Our model assumes the data lies on...' or 'We partition the data into...'. This leaves the foundational setup underspecified and forces the reader to infer the missing verb before proceeding.

comments.title: Missing adversarial objective equation
comments.quote: Both _G_ and _D_ engage in a two player min-max game where they try to find the Nash equilibrium for the following equation:
comments.explanation: The text explicitly introduces a minimax game and points directly to 'the following equation', but the passage immediately cuts to the Figure 2 caption. The mathematical formulation of the GAN loss (how instance features are incorporated into the real/fake losses, and how neighborhood sampling is weighted or aggregated) is completely omitted. Without this equation, a reader cannot understand the actual gradient updates or how the discriminator targets the local conditional density, which is a critical gap for method reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing subject and broken syntax in Introduction
comments.quote: This diversity of data points may result in a manifold with low density regions, which degrades the quality of the generated samples [11]. however, tends to deteriorate results [33, 36, 42] because the clusters may contain too few data points for the generator and discriminator to properly model their data distribution.
comments.explanation: The authors make a valid point about how data partitioning strategies impact GAN optimization. However, the second sentence begins with a lowercase 'however,' and completely lacks a grammatical subject (e.g., 'Finer partitions, however, tend to...'). This creates momentary ambiguity about what action is being described as detrimental. While context strongly suggests it refers to over-partitioning or finer clustering, the broken syntax prevents precise interpretation and disrupts the flow of the argument.

comments.title: Heavily corrupted mixture distribution formula
comments.explanation: The paper correctly attempts to frame IC-GAN as a non-parametric mixture density estimator analogous to KDE. However, the PDF extraction has severely garbled the approximation formula. The intended expression is undoubtedly $p(\mathbf{x}) \approx \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^M p(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{h}_i)$. While this is likely a standard OCR artifact, the current text presents an unreadable mathematical statement that breaks the formal link between the marginal distribution and the conditional components. Several other notation elements in the section (e.g., dataset indexing, universal quantifiers, and parameter subscripts) suffer from similar corruption, which undermines the rigor of the formal definitions and could mislead a reader attempting to implement the model.

comments.title: Notation imprecision: assigning class properties to feature vectors
comments.quote: This results in neighborhoods, where the number of neighbors belonging to the same class as the instance **h** _i_ is often smaller than _k_ .
comments.explanation: Initially, I paused at the phrase 'same class as the instance **h** _i_'. The symbol **h** _i_ is formally defined as a feature vector extracted by the embedding function _fφ_, not the image instance itself. While the intended meaning is clear (referring to the class label **y** _i_ associated with the original data point **x** _i_), technically it is a slight abuse of notation to attribute a class directly to the feature representation. The authors correctly define **h** _i_ = _fφ_( **x** _i_) earlier, so switching back to refer to the class of **h** _i_ rather than **x** _i_ is just a minor notational shortcut that could be tightened for precision.

comments.title: Ambiguity in class-conditional training versus inference conditioning
comments.quote: During training, real neighbors **x** _j_ and their respective labels **y** _j_ are sampled uniformly from _Ai_ for each **h** _i_ . In the class-conditional case, we model _p_ ( **x** _|_ **h** _i,_ **y** _j_ ) with a generator _G✓G_ ( **z** _,_ **h** _i,_ **y** _j_ ) trained jointly with a discriminator _D✓D_ ( **x** _,_ **h** _i,_ **y** _j_ ).
comments.explanation: The authors describe a training strategy where the class label **y** _j_ is drawn from a neighbor within the local neighborhood _Ai_. I checked the abstract and surrounding text, which mention 'semantically controllable generation', implying the model can handle arbitrary class inputs at inference. However, the training description only specifies sampling **y** _j_ from existing neighbors. A careful reader might initially wonder how the model handles class-conditional generation for a target class that does not appear in the neighbor set _Ai_, or how the discriminator learns to condition on **y** _j_ without conflicting with the visual style encoded in **h** _i_. While this is likely a standard trick to learn joint conditionals without explicit class-conditional training scaffolding, explicitly stating that **y** _j_ serves as a proxy to learn the full joint conditional _p_( **x** | **h**, **y**) over all available class embeddings would prevent confusion about inference capabilities and training dynamics.

comments.title: LPIPS metric scope and computation description
comments.quote: LPIPS computes the distance between the AlexNet activations of two images generated with two different latent vectors and same conditioning.
comments.explanation: I noted that the LPIPS metric is described specifically in the context of measuring diversity (fixed conditioning, different latent vectors). This is correct and well-aligned with common GAN evaluation practices. However, the phrasing 'distance between the AlexNet activations' is slightly imprecise; LPIPS computes a learned perceptual distance between the image-space representations processed through a network (typically VGG or AlexNet), comparing feature maps across multiple layers rather than just raw activation distances. The authors are using it correctly as a diversity metric here, but clarifying that it measures perceptual similarity/diversity in a multi-layer feature space rather than raw activations would align better with standard literature conventions. This is a minor framing issue that does not affect reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing preposition in evaluation protocol
comments.quote: On COCO-Stuff and ImageNet-LT, we compute the FID for each of the splits using all images in the split as reference, and sample the same number images.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'sample the same number images' is missing the word 'of'. This is a minor typographical oversight that does not affect the technical content or reproducibility, but should be corrected for grammatical correctness.

comments.title: Ambiguous channel multiplier notation in Table 1
comments.quote:
ch$	imes$ : Channel multiplier that affects network width as in BigGAN.

**IC-GAN**(_ch \times_64)
**IC-GAN**(_ch \times_64) + DA (d,i)
**IC-GAN**(_ch \times_96) + DA (d)
comments.explanation: The caption defines `ch$	imes$` as a channel multiplier, but the values `64` and `96` used in the table rows are unusually large for a typical BigGAN-style channel multiplier (which is usually a scalar like 1.0, 2.0, or 4.0). This is likely an OCR artifact or a notation inconsistency (e.g., intending to denote `x64` width multiplier, or `ch=64` base channels). Without clarification, a reproducer might incorrectly implement the network width. I flag this as a notation/typographical issue that could lead to configuration errors.

comments.title: Variance reporting limited to sampling noise seeds
comments.quote: All quantitative metrics for IC-GAN are reported over five random seeds for the input noise when sampling from the model.
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly state that standard deviations in the tables reflect variance only over five noise seeds during sampling, not over different training initializations or data shuffles. While transparent, this is a methodological choice that significantly underestimates the true variance of the training process. Readers accustomed to standard reporting conventions might mistakenly assume the reported error bars capture training instability or hyperparameter sensitivity. It would be more informative to at least mention this limitation or clarify that training variance is negligible or not of primary interest.

comments.title: Cryptic abbreviation in Table 1 caption
comments.quote: DA: 50% horizontal flips in (d) real and fake samples, and (i) conditioning instances.
comments.explanation: The caption uses `(d)` and `(i)` to denote which inputs receive data augmentation, but does not explicitly define what `(d)` and `(i)` stand for. While they can be inferred from the table rows (`IC-GAN + DA (d,i)`), a reader scanning the caption might find the abbreviations unclear. Explicitly stating something like '(d): data samples, (i): conditioning instances' would improve clarity without adding length.

comments.title: Missing adversarial objective equation
comments.quote:
Both G and D engage in a two player min-max game where they try to find the Nash equilibrium for the following equation:

3
comments.explanation: I initially noticed the text explicitly introduces a min-max game and promises an equation, but only a page number follows. This raised a concern about whether the training objective was omitted or misplaced. I checked the surrounding context and the summary, which confirms the authors use a standard adversarial setup where the discriminator distinguishes real neighbors from generated samples conditioned on the same instance. While this is almost certainly a PDF page-break artifact in the OCR extraction, the equation itself is genuinely missing from the provided text. Without the explicit loss function (e.g., Wasserstein, standard GAN, or hinge loss), the exact adversarial dynamics, gradient flows, and how the discriminator handles multiple real neighbors per instance remain underspecified. A careful reader cannot verify the training procedure or reproduce the setup without making assumptions about the loss formulation.

comments.title: LPIPS interpretation as a diversity metric may mislead
comments.quote: IC-GAN exhibits notably higher LPIPS than LostGANv2 and OC-GAN, which could be explained by the fact that the latter only leverage one real sample per input conditioning during training; whereas IC-GAN uses multiple real neighboring samples per each instance, naturally favouring diversity in the generated images.
comments.explanation: At first glance, claiming that 'higher LPIPS' is desirable immediately struck me as contradictory, since LPIPS (Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity) is conventionally treated as a distance metric where lower values indicate better alignment or quality. I verified how the metric is used in the evaluation protocol (Section 3.1), which notes that LPIPS computes distances between activations of two images generated with different latents but the same conditioning. In GAN literature, computing LPIPS across a set of generated samples to measure pairwise dissimilarity is a valid way to quantify diversity, where higher scores indeed indicate more diverse outputs. However, because this directionality flips the standard 'lower is better' heuristic, a careful reader unfamiliar with this specific evaluation convention could reasonably misinterpret the result as degraded image quality or distribution mismatch. The authors' intent is correct, but the phrasing lacks the explicit caveat that LPIPS here measures intra-set perceptual dissimilarity, which could mislead a broader audience.

comments.title: Inconsistent recommendation of neighborhood size k across datasets
comments.quote: Overall, k = 50 offers a good compromise, exhibiting top performance across all metrics when using at least 500 stored instances.
comments.explanation: I first checked the experimental setup in Section 3.1, where the authors state they set k = 50 for ImageNet but explicitly chose k = 5 for both COCO-Stuff and ImageNet-LT. Later, in the neighborhood size analysis (Section 3.5), they conclude that k = 50 offers a good compromise and exhibits top performance. This creates a slight tension: if k = 50 is generally superior, why was k = 5 selected for the other datasets in the main experiments? I considered whether dataset-specific manifold densities or computational constraints might justify the lower k, but the text does not provide this rationale. The omission leaves the hyperparameter tuning strategy partially underspecified and may confuse readers attempting to transfer the method to new domains, as the guideline conflicts with the reported dataset-specific settings.

comments.title: Unaddressed implication of uniform weights in overlapping neighborhoods
comments.quote: Our objective is to model the underlying data distribution p( x ) as a mixture of conditional distributions p( x | h i ) around each of M instance feature vectors h i in the dataset, such that p( x ) ≈ 1/M ∑_i p( x | h i ).
comments.explanation: The authors approximate the global density as a uniform mixture over M instance conditionals, explicitly noting that the neighborhoods are overlapping. I initially wondered whether overlapping partitions would cause certain regions of the manifold to be over-represented (since they belong to multiple neighborhoods) while sparse regions are under-represented. In classical KDE, overlap is naturally handled by kernel bandwidths and often weighted by distance or density estimates. The authors do not acknowledge or justify why uniform mixing weights are sufficient despite the explicit overlap. While this is a common and practical simplification, omitting the assumption means the theoretical grounding is slightly loose: the approximation error heavily depends on neighborhood uniformity, which is dataset-dependent. For a method positioning itself as a non-parametric density estimator, a brief note on how uniform weights interact with overlap would strengthen the justification.

comments.title: Contradiction between neighborhood size $k$ and its claimed effect on generation diversity
comments.quote:
Summary: "Neighborhood size $k$ acts as a KDE-like bandwidth parameter: smaller $k$ enhances generation diversity but requires more stored instances, while larger $k$ yields smoother conditional distributions needing fewer instances."

Section 3.3: "Transferring the model from ImageNet boosts diversity w.r.t. the model trained on COCO-Stuff (see LPIPS in Table 2), which may be in part due to the larger $k=50$ used for ImageNet training, compared to $k=5$ when training on COCO-Stuff."
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by two statements that appear to assign opposite effects to the neighborhood size $k$ on generation diversity. In the summary, the authors explicitly state that 'smaller $k$ enhances generation diversity,' drawing an analogy to KDE bandwidth where a tighter neighborhood captures sharper, more localized modes. However, in Section 3.3, they attribute increased diversity (quantified via higher LPIPS, which measures perceptual distance between samples conditioned on the same instance) to the *larger* $k=50$ used during ImageNet training, contrasted with $k=5$ for COCO-Stuff. I checked the LPIPS definition in Section 3.1 to confirm it measures intra-conditioning variation, validating that higher LPIPS indeed corresponds to greater diversity. I also considered whether 'diversity' might be used ambiguously to mean dataset coverage/mode coverage in the summary versus within-condition variation in Section 3.3, but the direct causal phrasing ('due to the larger $k=50$...') creates a logical conflict. It remains unclear whether smaller neighborhoods increase diversity by forcing the mixture to cover more distinct modes across the dataset, or whether larger neighborhoods improve diversity by providing the discriminator with a richer, more varied set of real samples during adversarial training. The authors should clarify the mechanism and reconcile these statements.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and well-motivated approach to unconditional image generation by framing the problem as a mixture of local densities, inspired by kernel density estimation. The writing is clear and the connection to non-parametric methods effectively justifies the instance-conditioning design. A key strength is the demonstration of zero-shot transfer to unseen datasets, which highlights the generalizability of the learned local generative processes. However, while the overall idea is elegant, the paper could benefit from a more rigorous analysis of the sensitivity to the neighborhood size and the choice of feature space used for nearest-neighbor retrieval. The current description (in Section 2) suggests these are fixed, yet they likely have a large impact on the trade-off between local fidelity and global coverage. Additionally, the distinction between IC-GAN and other cluster-based GANs is clear in principle, but the paper would be strengthened by a more detailed discussion of how overlapping neighborhoods alleviate the small-cluster problem in practice, especially for datasets with highly variable local density. Overall, the paper is of high quality and clarity, with a significant contribution; the most notable issues are the need for more thorough ablation on the conditioning hyperparameters and a deeper exploration of the limitations when the target domain diverges markedly from the training distribution.

comments.title: Unfinished or garbled sentence in Introduction
comments.quote: This diversity of data points may result in a manifold with low density regions, which degrades the quality of the generated samples [11]. however, tends to deteriorate results [33, 36, 42] because the clusters may contain too few data points for the generator and discriminator to properly model their data distribution.
comments.explanation: The sentence beginning with 'however' appears to be a fragment or is missing a subject. The preceding sentence discusses low-density regions degrading quality; the next fragment seems to discuss a different issue (clusters with too few data points). The transition is abrupt and the grammatical structure is incomplete. Given the context of discussing drawbacks of coarse, non-overlapping partitions versus overly small clusters, the intended meaning can be inferred, but the text as written is ungrammatical and could confuse a reader.

comments.title: Possible missing negation or clarification in KDE similarity comparison
comments.quote: the kernel in IC-GAN is no longer independent on the data point on which we condition
comments.explanation: The phrase 'no longer independent on' is ambiguous. It likely means the kernel is not independent of the data point (i.e., it depends on it), which contrasts with standard KDE where the kernel shape is typically the same for all points. However, 'no longer independent on' could be read as 'is dependent on', but the phrasing is awkward. A more precise wording would be 'no longer independent of' or 'depends on'. This is a minor language issue and the intended meaning is clear from context.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing about 'poor quality and/or low diversity' in unconditional GANs
comments.quote: GANs present optimization difficulties and can suffer from mode collapse, resulting in the generator not being able to obtain a good distribution coverage, and often producing poor quality and/or low diversity generated samples.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'poor quality and/or low diversity' is slightly ambiguous in the context of mode collapse. Mode collapse typically reduces diversity but can also degrade quality; however, the ordering 'not being able to obtain a good distribution coverage, and often producing poor quality and/or low diversity' could be read as two separate consequences. A careful reader might wonder if mode collapse leads to both poor quality and low diversity, or one or the other. This is a minor clarity issue and does not affect the scientific content.

comments.title: Ambiguous mixture density formula
comments.explanation: The typeset formula appears to be a garbled OCR rendering of the intended expression p(x) ≈ (1/M) ∑ᵢ p(x|hᵢ).  The OCR has inserted stray brackets and spaces, and the fraction '1/M' is written as 'M 1'.  However, the surrounding prose and the overall meaning are clear: the authors are modeling the data distribution as an equally weighted mixture of conditional densities.  Since this is a commonly understood notation and the prose states the same idea ('mixture of conditional distributions'), the OCR corruption is not a substantive error.  No issue remains.

comments.title: OCR corruption in dataset definition
comments.quote: D = { x_i}[M] i =1[with][ M][data samples][ x][i][and an embedding] function f
comments.explanation: The OCR has mangled the standard set notation: the intended text is likely 'D = {x_i}_{i=1}^M with M data samples x_i and an embedding function f'.  The bracketed insertions and misaligned brackets make the sentence hard to parse at first glance, but a reader familiar with the field will immediately reconstruct the intended meaning.  This is a formatting artifact, not a substantive error or ambiguity that would mislead a careful reader.  No issue remains.

comments.title: OCR corruption in labeled dataset definition
comments.quote: Dl = { ( x_i, y_i ) }[M] i =1[with][ M][data sample pairs][ (][x][i,][ y][i][)][ and an embedding function] fφ
comments.explanation: Same OCR-induced typesetting issue as above: the set/sequence notation is broken.  The intended content is clear: the labeled dataset consists of M pairs (x_i, y_i).  The rest of the paragraph correctly describes the nearest-neighbor procedure.  This is a formatting glitch, not a substantive error.  No issue remains.

comments.title: Generator equation is cut off
comments.quote: Both G and D engage in a two player min-max game where they try to find the Nash equilibrium for the following equation:
comments.explanation: The passage tells the reader that a min-max game equation follows, but the actual equation is not shown — instead, the section ends with a page break and '3' (the page number).  This is clearly a truncation of the extracted text, not an author error.  The equation likely appears on the next page of the PDF.  Since the OCR cutoff is at a passage boundary, this does not constitute an error in the paper itself.  No issue remains.

comments.title: Potential notation inconsistency: 'real neighbors x_j and their respective labels y_j' in class-conditional setting
comments.quote: During training, real neighbors x_j and their respective labels y_j are sampled uniformly from A_i for each h_i. In the class-conditional case, we model p( x | h_i, y_j ) with a generator G_θ_G ( z , h_i, y_j )
comments.explanation: At first glance, the conditioning on y_j (the neighbor's label) rather than y_i (the instance's own label) is surprising: one might expect the model to generate images of the same class as the conditioning instance.  However, the authors note earlier that neighborhoods may contain instances from multiple classes, and they explicitly define the conditional distribution as p(x | h_i, y_j).  This is a deliberate design choice: the generator is required to produce images matching the class of the real neighbor sample, not the class of the conditioning instance.  The notation is consistent within the paragraph and with the intention of modeling local multi-class neighborhoods.  The ambiguity is resolved by context.  No issue remains.

comments.title: Underspecified: how many stored instances are used at inference time for transfer experiments?
comments.quote: Unless stated otherwise, we store 1,000 training set instances by applying k-means clustering to the training set and selecting the features of the data point that is the closest to each one of the centroids.
comments.explanation: This description of selecting 1,000 stored instances is given in Section 3.1 (Evaluation protocol) for standard evaluation.  However, the transfer experiments in Section 3.3 ('Off-the-shelf transfer to other datasets') use conditioning instances from other datasets (e.g., COCO-Stuff, Cityscapes).  The paper does not specify whether, for transfer, (a) the same 1,000 ImageNet k-means centroids are used, (b) a fresh k-means clustering is applied to the target dataset, or (c) all target instances are used directly.  The phrase 'Unless stated otherwise' suggests the 1,000 stored-instance rule is the default, but the transfer setting is a non-default usage.  The authors appear to condition on instances from the target dataset (e.g., 'IC-GAN trained on ImageNet outperforms the same model trained on COCO-Stuff for all splits'), which implies they used COCO-Stuff instances — but are these the full set (76k images) or a subsample?  This lack of specification could affect reproducibility.  However, the paper later notes that 'storing' refers to selecting a subset for efficiency, and the downstream transfer results are still meaningful regardless of the exact number of stored instances, as long as the same protocol is applied to both IC-GAN and baselines.  The ambiguity is moderate but does not undermine the main claims.

comments.title: Unclear why all training datapoints are used for conditioning but only 1,000 stored for evaluation
comments.quote: Note that when training IC-GAN we use all available training datapoints to condition the model. ... Unless stated otherwise, we store 1,000 training set instances by applying k-means clustering
comments.explanation: The passage initially states that during training 'all available training datapoints' condition the model, but then the evaluation section describes using only 1,000 stored (clustered) instances for sampling.  This might appear contradictory: why not use the same set of instances for both training and evaluation?  The intent is likely that during training, the model sees all instances (sampled each mini-batch), but for efficient metric computation, only a subset of representative instances is used to generate samples.  This is a practical decision common in non-parametric models.  The text is not explicit about why the reduction is made, but a careful reader in the field would recognize the trade-off between coverage and compute cost.  The ambiguity is minor and resolvable by context.

comments.title: Inconsistent instance storage for large-scale generation near top-5 performance
comments.quote: Unless stated otherwise, we store 1,000 training set instances by applying k-means clustering to the training set and selecting the features of the data point that is the closest to each one of the centroids.
comments.explanation: The claim that IC-GAN is analogous to kernel density estimation might suggest storing many (if not all) instance features, but only 1,000 centroids (or 1,000 cluster-representative instances) are used for sampling at inference. Initially this seemed like a severe undersampling of the data manifold. However, context partly resolves the concern because the evaluation protocol (Sec 3.1) notes that all quantitative metrics are reported over five random seeds with this 1,000-instance store. The passage does note 'Unless stated otherwise', and Table 1 and other experiments (e.g., 50k generated samples for FID) might be consistently produced using these 1,000 stored instances. Still, for a 1.2M-image dataset like ImageNet with 1,000 classes, using only 1,000 instances (average one per class) as conditioning support is surprisingly small and could be a serious limitation (or a clever trick). The authors do not explicitly justify why 1,000 is sufficient or robust, and the reader could mistakenly think the model requires all training instances. This underspecification is methodologically questionable.

comments.title: Reference to Table 8 before it is introduced
comments.quote: Additional results in terms of precision and recall can be found in Table 8 in the supplementary material.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, after discussing ImageNet results, the text says 'Additional results in terms of precision and recall can be found in Table 8 in the supplementary material.' This is a forward reference to a table in supplementary material, which is acceptable for a NeurIPS paper. However, the phrasing assumes the reader has access to the supplementary material to verify a claim ('additional results'). While not an error, it is ambiguous whether the authors are implying that precision/recall metrics are favorable (which would strengthen the claim that IC-GAN avoids mode collapse) or just reporting them neutrally. The claim in context is only that IC-GAN 'generates high quality images preserving most of the appearance of the conditioning instance', not that it has high recall; the reference to Table 8 could mislead a careful reader into thinking supplementary data supports that claim, but it is not provided. This is minor overclaim by implication.

comments.title: Ambiguity about class-conditional extension's compatibility with unlabeled feature extractor
comments.quote: For the class-conditional IC-GAN, we use a ResNet50 trained for the classification task on either ImageNet or ImageNetLT [26].
comments.explanation: The paper earlier states that the feature extractor is 'learned in an unsupervised or self-supervised manner' for the unlabeled scenario. Here, for the class-conditional case, a supervised feature extractor (trained on ImageNet or ImageNet-LT) is used. This is consistent with the class-conditional extension described in Section 2, where the embedding function can be learned in a 'supervised manner'. However, the passage does not explicitly state that the same supervised extractor is used for the class-conditional experiments in Table 3 (not shown in context but implied). A careful reader might wonder whether the class-conditional IC-GAN benefits from a supervised feature extractor that could see labels, which might improve nearest-neighbor quality. The paper does not justify why this choice is appropriate or if it gives an unfair advantage over methods like BigGAN that do not use extra supervised features. This is an underspecified methodological detail that could affect reproducibility.

comments.title: Neighborhood size discrepancy between training and inference for transfer
comments.quote: Unless stated otherwise, we set the size of the neighborhoods to k =50 for ImageNet and k =5 for both COCO-Stuff and ImageNet-LT.
comments.explanation: The passage states that for ImageNet, k=50, while for COCO-Stuff and ImageNet-LT, k=5. However, when IC-GAN is transferred from ImageNet to COCO-Stuff (Section 3.3), the model is trained on ImageNet with k=50 but applied to COCO-Stuff instances. The paper does not explicitly state whether the neighborhood size used during the transfer sampling (inference) is the same as the training-time k (50) or if it is adjusted to match the target dataset (e.g., k=5). The term 'neighborhood' has two meanings: (1) the set of training neighbors used as real samples during training, and (2) the set of nearest neighbors of the conditioning instance at inference (which may come from a different dataset). The paper does not specify if at inference the same k is used to retrieve neighbors from the stored instances (which would be inconsistent if the target dataset has different density). This ambiguity could lead a reader to incorrectly assume the same neighborhood size is used throughout, which could affect diversity metrics like LPIPS.

comments.title: Claim of outperforming all previous approaches at 64x64 and 128x128 but includes non-standard baselines
comments.quote: IC-GAN surpasses all previous approaches at both 64x64 and 128x128 resolutions in both FID and IS scores.
comments.explanation: The claim is strong, but the table includes a baseline ('Uncond. BigGAN') that is trained by 'setting all labels to zero', which is a non-standard approach. The authors note that this is a re-implemented baseline following [36,42], and the FID/IS scores for these baselines are marked with asterisks indicating they are computed with PyTorch rather than TensorFlow. The claim might be technically correct within the reported table, but the reader should be aware that some baseline scores may not be directly comparable to official implementations. Moreover, at 256x256 resolution, the only competitor listed is ADM (a diffusion model), which is a concurrent work. The claim of 'outperforming' at lower resolutions may be justified, but the authors do not discuss potential differences in evaluation frameworks (PyTorch vs TensorFlow) that could affect numerical comparisons. This is a minor issue of overclaim without caveat.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in description of neighborhood size analysis
comments.quote: For small numbers of stored instances, we observe that larger the neighborhoods lead to better (lower) FID scores (left-hand side of left panel). For recall, we also observe improvements for large neighborhoods when storing few instances (left-hand side of right panel), suggesting that larger neighborhoods are more effective in recovering the data distribution from few instances.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'larger the neighborhoods lead to better (lower) FID scores' has a grammatical issue that could cause confusion. More importantly, the prose states that 'larger neighborhoods are more effective in recovering the data distribution from few instances.' However, earlier in the paper (Section 3.5 is described as analyzing neighborhood size, but only this excerpt is provided), the authors claim that smaller k (e.g., 5) results in lower diversity. Here they argue larger neighborhoods help with few instances, which is clear. But the next sentence says 'This trend is reverted for large numbers of stored instances, where smaller values of k are more effective.' This reversal is a key observation. The issue is that the authors do not provide a mechanistic explanation for why the trend reverses, which is acceptable for a discussion section, but the phrasing 'supports the idea that the neighborhood size acts as a bandwidth parameter – similar to KDE –, that controls the smoothness' is somewhat vague. While KDE bandwidth controls bias-variance tradeoff, the exact analogy here is not fully justified. The claim is plausible but underspecified — what does 'smoothness' mean operationally for the conditional distributions learned by the GAN? This is a minor framing concern rather than an error.

comments.title: Ambiguous comparison between FID and recall in precision-recall analysis
comments.quote: Moreover, as expected, we notice that the value of k does not significantly affect precision (right panel).
comments.explanation: The authors refer to 'precision (right panel)' but the preceding sentences discuss FID and recall in the left and right panels of Figure 4, which is not shown in this excerpt. From the text, it appears the right panel shows recall (and possibly precision). The claim that k does not significantly affect precision is stated as 'as expected,' but without seeing the figure or a derivation, it is unclear why this is expected. The earlier discussion (in the paper summary) notes that increasing stored instances improves recall at the expense of slightly worse precision. Here, the claim is about k, not number of instances. The relationship between k and precision is not obviously intuitive; readers may need more justification. However, this is a minor ambiguity since it is plausible and the authors provide a qualitative statement without claiming rigorous proof.

comments.title: Potential overclaim in transfer capabilities description
comments.quote: Perhaps surprisingly, IC-GAN trained on ImageNet outperforms the same model trained on COCO-Stuff for all splits: 8.5 vs. 16.8 train FID at 128 resolution. This raises the question of how close ImageNet and COCO-Stuff data distributions are. We compute the FID between real data train split of the two datasets at 128×128 resolution and obtain a score of 37.2. Hence, the remarkable transfer capabilities of IC-GAN are not explained by dataset similarity and may be attributed to the effectiveness of the ImageNet pre-trained feature extractor and generator.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the FID of 37.2 between the two datasets indicates low similarity, so the transfer success must be due to the pre-trained feature extractor and generator. However, FID measures distribution distance in Inception feature space; a score of 37.2 is not extremely high — for reference, FID between different ImageNet classes can be much larger. The authors do not provide a baseline FID for unrelated datasets or a threshold for 'similar.' The reasoning that because FID is 37.2 the datasets are dissimilar, thus the transfer is 'remarkable,' is somewhat circular. The claim may be overstated, though not necessarily incorrect. A moderate concern: the argument that 'not explained by dataset similarity' is insufficiently justified without a calibrated notion of what FID score indicates strong dissimilarity in the context of transfer learning.

comments.title: Missing standard deviation entries in Table 2
comments.explanation: In the 128×128 section of Table 2, the row for 'IC-GAN (BigGAN, transf.)' reports FID and LPIPS values with ± standard deviations, but while other rows (e.g., LostGANv2, Unconditional (BigGAN), IC-GAN (BigGAN)) report standard deviations for all four FID columns (train, eval, eval seen, eval unseen), this row reports standard deviations only for the eval seen and eval unseen columns. The train and eval columns show only a single value (8.5 and 35.6) with ±0.0 for the LPIPS column. This is inconsistent: either the standard deviations for train and eval FID are missing (should be reported with ± notation) or they were omitted because they are zero, but the table formatting suggests they may have been inadvertently dropped. The authors' intent was clearly to report all metrics with standard deviations, and the summary states 'All quantitative metrics for IC-GAN are reported over five random seeds', so the omission stands out.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and well-motivated approach to unconditional image generation by framing the problem as a mixture of local densities, inspired by kernel density estimation. The writing is clear and the connection to non-parametric methods effectively justifies the instance-conditioning design. A key strength is the demonstration of zero-shot transfer to unseen datasets, which highlights the generalizability of the learned local generative processes. However, while the overall idea is elegant, the paper could benefit from a more rigorous analysis of the sensitivity to the neighborhood size and the choice of feature space used for nearest-neighbor retrieval. The current description (in Section 2) suggests these are fixed, yet they likely have a large impact on the trade-off between local fidelity and global coverage. Additionally, the distinction between IC-GAN and other cluster-based GANs is clear in principle, but the paper would be strengthened by a more detailed discussion of how overlapping neighborhoods alleviate the small-cluster problem in practice, especially for datasets with highly variable local density. Overall, the paper is of high quality and clarity, with a significant contribution; the most notable issues are the need for more thorough ablation on the conditioning hyperparameters and a deeper exploration of the limitations when the target domain diverges markedly from the training distribution.

comments.title: Unfinished or garbled sentence in Introduction
comments.quote: This diversity of data points may result in a manifold with low density regions, which degrades the quality of the generated samples [11]. however, tends to deteriorate results [33, 36, 42] because the clusters may contain too few data points for the generator and discriminator to properly model their data distribution.
comments.explanation: The sentence beginning with 'however' appears to be a fragment or is missing a subject. The preceding sentence discusses low-density regions degrading quality; the next fragment seems to discuss a different issue (clusters with too few data points). The transition is abrupt and the grammatical structure is incomplete. Given the context of discussing drawbacks of coarse, non-overlapping partitions versus overly small clusters, the intended meaning can be inferred, but the text as written is ungrammatical and could confuse a reader.

comments.title: Possible missing negation or clarification in KDE similarity comparison
comments.quote: the kernel in IC-GAN is no longer independent on the data point on which we condition
comments.explanation: The phrase 'no longer independent on' is ambiguous. It likely means the kernel is not independent of the data point (i.e., it depends on it), which contrasts with standard KDE where the kernel shape is typically the same for all points. However, 'no longer independent on' could be read as 'is dependent on', but the phrasing is awkward. A more precise wording would be 'no longer independent of' or 'depends on'. This is a minor language issue and the intended meaning is clear from context.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing about 'poor quality and/or low diversity' in unconditional GANs
comments.quote: GANs present optimization difficulties and can suffer from mode collapse, resulting in the generator not being able to obtain a good distribution coverage, and often producing poor quality and/or low diversity generated samples.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'poor quality and/or low diversity' is slightly ambiguous in the context of mode collapse. Mode collapse typically reduces diversity but can also degrade quality; however, the ordering 'not being able to obtain a good distribution coverage, and often producing poor quality and/or low diversity' could be read as two separate consequences. A careful reader might wonder if mode collapse leads to both poor quality and low diversity, or one or the other. This is a minor clarity issue and does not affect the scientific content.

comments.title: Ambiguous mixture density formula
comments.explanation: The typeset formula appears to be a garbled OCR rendering of the intended expression p(x) ≈ (1/M) ∑ᵢ p(x|hᵢ).  The OCR has inserted stray brackets and spaces, and the fraction '1/M' is written as 'M 1'.  However, the surrounding prose and the overall meaning are clear: the authors are modeling the data distribution as an equally weighted mixture of conditional densities.  Since this is a commonly understood notation and the prose states the same idea ('mixture of conditional distributions'), the OCR corruption is not a substantive error.  No issue remains.

comments.title: OCR corruption in dataset definition
comments.quote: D = { x_i}[M] i =1[with][ M][data samples][ x][i][and an embedding] function f
comments.explanation: The OCR has mangled the standard set notation: the intended text is likely 'D = {x_i}_{i=1}^M with M data samples x_i and an embedding function f'.  The bracketed insertions and misaligned brackets make the sentence hard to parse at first glance, but a reader familiar with the field will immediately reconstruct the intended meaning.  This is a formatting artifact, not a substantive error or ambiguity that would mislead a careful reader.  No issue remains.

comments.title: OCR corruption in labeled dataset definition
comments.quote: Dl = { ( x_i, y_i ) }[M] i =1[with][ M][data sample pairs][ (][x][i,][ y][i][)][ and an embedding function] fφ
comments.explanation: Same OCR-induced typesetting issue as above: the set/sequence notation is broken.  The intended content is clear: the labeled dataset consists of M pairs (x_i, y_i).  The rest of the paragraph correctly describes the nearest-neighbor procedure.  This is a formatting glitch, not a substantive error.  No issue remains.

comments.title: Generator equation is cut off
comments.quote: Both G and D engage in a two player min-max game where they try to find the Nash equilibrium for the following equation:
comments.explanation: The passage tells the reader that a min-max game equation follows, but the actual equation is not shown — instead, the section ends with a page break and '3' (the page number).  This is clearly a truncation of the extracted text, not an author error.  The equation likely appears on the next page of the PDF.  Since the OCR cutoff is at a passage boundary, this does not constitute an error in the paper itself.  No issue remains.

comments.title: Potential notation inconsistency: 'real neighbors x_j and their respective labels y_j' in class-conditional setting
comments.quote: During training, real neighbors x_j and their respective labels y_j are sampled uniformly from A_i for each h_i. In the class-conditional case, we model p( x | h_i, y_j ) with a generator G_θ_G ( z , h_i, y_j )
comments.explanation: At first glance, the conditioning on y_j (the neighbor's label) rather than y_i (the instance's own label) is surprising: one might expect the model to generate images of the same class as the conditioning instance.  However, the authors note earlier that neighborhoods may contain instances from multiple classes, and they explicitly define the conditional distribution as p(x | h_i, y_j).  This is a deliberate design choice: the generator is required to produce images matching the class of the real neighbor sample, not the class of the conditioning instance.  The notation is consistent within the paragraph and with the intention of modeling local multi-class neighborhoods.  The ambiguity is resolved by context.  No issue remains.

comments.title: Underspecified: how many stored instances are used at inference time for transfer experiments?
comments.quote: Unless stated otherwise, we store 1,000 training set instances by applying k-means clustering to the training set and selecting the features of the data point that is the closest to each one of the centroids.
comments.explanation: This description of selecting 1,000 stored instances is given in Section 3.1 (Evaluation protocol) for standard evaluation.  However, the transfer experiments in Section 3.3 ('Off-the-shelf transfer to other datasets') use conditioning instances from other datasets (e.g., COCO-Stuff, Cityscapes).  The paper does not specify whether, for transfer, (a) the same 1,000 ImageNet k-means centroids are used, (b) a fresh k-means clustering is applied to the target dataset, or (c) all target instances are used directly.  The phrase 'Unless stated otherwise' suggests the 1,000 stored-instance rule is the default, but the transfer setting is a non-default usage.  The authors appear to condition on instances from the target dataset (e.g., 'IC-GAN trained on ImageNet outperforms the same model trained on COCO-Stuff for all splits'), which implies they used COCO-Stuff instances — but are these the full set (76k images) or a subsample?  This lack of specification could affect reproducibility.  However, the paper later notes that 'storing' refers to selecting a subset for efficiency, and the downstream transfer results are still meaningful regardless of the exact number of stored instances, as long as the same protocol is applied to both IC-GAN and baselines.  The ambiguity is moderate but does not undermine the main claims.

comments.title: Unclear why all training datapoints are used for conditioning but only 1,000 stored for evaluation
comments.quote: Note that when training IC-GAN we use all available training datapoints to condition the model. ... Unless stated otherwise, we store 1,000 training set instances by applying k-means clustering
comments.explanation: The passage initially states that during training 'all available training datapoints' condition the model, but then the evaluation section describes using only 1,000 stored (clustered) instances for sampling.  This might appear contradictory: why not use the same set of instances for both training and evaluation?  The intent is likely that during training, the model sees all instances (sampled each mini-batch), but for efficient metric computation, only a subset of representative instances is used to generate samples.  This is a practical decision common in non-parametric models.  The text is not explicit about why the reduction is made, but a careful reader in the field would recognize the trade-off between coverage and compute cost.  The ambiguity is minor and resolvable by context.

comments.title: Inconsistent instance storage for large-scale generation near top-5 performance
comments.quote: Unless stated otherwise, we store 1,000 training set instances by applying k-means clustering to the training set and selecting the features of the data point that is the closest to each one of the centroids.
comments.explanation: The claim that IC-GAN is analogous to kernel density estimation might suggest storing many (if not all) instance features, but only 1,000 centroids (or 1,000 cluster-representative instances) are used for sampling at inference. Initially this seemed like a severe undersampling of the data manifold. However, context partly resolves the concern because the evaluation protocol (Sec 3.1) notes that all quantitative metrics are reported over five random seeds with this 1,000-instance store. The passage does note 'Unless stated otherwise', and Table 1 and other experiments (e.g., 50k generated samples for FID) might be consistently produced using these 1,000 stored instances. Still, for a 1.2M-image dataset like ImageNet with 1,000 classes, using only 1,000 instances (average one per class) as conditioning support is surprisingly small and could be a serious limitation (or a clever trick). The authors do not explicitly justify why 1,000 is sufficient or robust, and the reader could mistakenly think the model requires all training instances. This underspecification is methodologically questionable.

comments.title: Reference to Table 8 before it is introduced
comments.quote: Additional results in terms of precision and recall can be found in Table 8 in the supplementary material.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, after discussing ImageNet results, the text says 'Additional results in terms of precision and recall can be found in Table 8 in the supplementary material.' This is a forward reference to a table in supplementary material, which is acceptable for a NeurIPS paper. However, the phrasing assumes the reader has access to the supplementary material to verify a claim ('additional results'). While not an error, it is ambiguous whether the authors are implying that precision/recall metrics are favorable (which would strengthen the claim that IC-GAN avoids mode collapse) or just reporting them neutrally. The claim in context is only that IC-GAN 'generates high quality images preserving most of the appearance of the conditioning instance', not that it has high recall; the reference to Table 8 could mislead a careful reader into thinking supplementary data supports that claim, but it is not provided. This is minor overclaim by implication.

comments.title: Ambiguity about class-conditional extension's compatibility with unlabeled feature extractor
comments.quote: For the class-conditional IC-GAN, we use a ResNet50 trained for the classification task on either ImageNet or ImageNetLT [26].
comments.explanation: The paper earlier states that the feature extractor is 'learned in an unsupervised or self-supervised manner' for the unlabeled scenario. Here, for the class-conditional case, a supervised feature extractor (trained on ImageNet or ImageNet-LT) is used. This is consistent with the class-conditional extension described in Section 2, where the embedding function can be learned in a 'supervised manner'. However, the passage does not explicitly state that the same supervised extractor is used for the class-conditional experiments in Table 3 (not shown in context but implied). A careful reader might wonder whether the class-conditional IC-GAN benefits from a supervised feature extractor that could see labels, which might improve nearest-neighbor quality. The paper does not justify why this choice is appropriate or if it gives an unfair advantage over methods like BigGAN that do not use extra supervised features. This is an underspecified methodological detail that could affect reproducibility.

comments.title: Neighborhood size discrepancy between training and inference for transfer
comments.quote: Unless stated otherwise, we set the size of the neighborhoods to k =50 for ImageNet and k =5 for both COCO-Stuff and ImageNet-LT.
comments.explanation: The passage states that for ImageNet, k=50, while for COCO-Stuff and ImageNet-LT, k=5. However, when IC-GAN is transferred from ImageNet to COCO-Stuff (Section 3.3), the model is trained on ImageNet with k=50 but applied to COCO-Stuff instances. The paper does not explicitly state whether the neighborhood size used during the transfer sampling (inference) is the same as the training-time k (50) or if it is adjusted to match the target dataset (e.g., k=5). The term 'neighborhood' has two meanings: (1) the set of training neighbors used as real samples during training, and (2) the set of nearest neighbors of the conditioning instance at inference (which may come from a different dataset). The paper does not specify if at inference the same k is used to retrieve neighbors from the stored instances (which would be inconsistent if the target dataset has different density). This ambiguity could lead a reader to incorrectly assume the same neighborhood size is used throughout, which could affect diversity metrics like LPIPS.

comments.title: Claim of outperforming all previous approaches at 64x64 and 128x128 but includes non-standard baselines
comments.quote: IC-GAN surpasses all previous approaches at both 64x64 and 128x128 resolutions in both FID and IS scores.
comments.explanation: The claim is strong, but the table includes a baseline ('Uncond. BigGAN') that is trained by 'setting all labels to zero', which is a non-standard approach. The authors note that this is a re-implemented baseline following [36,42], and the FID/IS scores for these baselines are marked with asterisks indicating they are computed with PyTorch rather than TensorFlow. The claim might be technically correct within the reported table, but the reader should be aware that some baseline scores may not be directly comparable to official implementations. Moreover, at 256x256 resolution, the only competitor listed is ADM (a diffusion model), which is a concurrent work. The claim of 'outperforming' at lower resolutions may be justified, but the authors do not discuss potential differences in evaluation frameworks (PyTorch vs TensorFlow) that could affect numerical comparisons. This is a minor issue of overclaim without caveat.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents **Instance-Conditioned GAN (IC-GAN)**, a compelling framework that attempts to bridge unsupervised generative modeling and non-parametric density estimation (KDE) to overcome mode collapse in complex distributions. The authors' central insight—that modeling the local distribution of the data manifold centered on a specific "instance" rather than coarse global clusters—provides a novel mechanism for handling high-dimensional datasets like ImageNet without labeled supervision. The manuscript demonstrates impressive generalization capabilities, showing that the model can transfer to unseen datasets simply by conditioning on different instances. While the motivation and clarity regarding the overlapping neighborhood strategy are strong, a significant concern arises regarding the computational overhead of retrieving nearest neighbors at inference time, which is critical for the practical viability of this approach on large-scale datasets. The proposed method clearly improves upon baselines, but the scalability analysis will determine its eventual impact.

comments.title: Incorrect numerical value in Abstract due to OCR
comments.explanation: The text presents the FID score as '210.4*_±_0.1', which is likely an OCR error where a preceding character or column boundary was misaligned, causing the '2' to be attached to '10.4'. The context (Table 1) correctly lists this value as '10.4*_±_0.1' for the 64x64 Unconditional BigGAN model. This error affects the reported baseline score.

comments.title: Garbled resolution notation in Abstract
comments.quote: c. (m ⇥ 256 Resolution
comments.explanation: The text contains 'm ⇥ 256' which appears to be a corrupted rendering of the resolution '256 _⇥_ 256'. The 'c.' likely originated from a caption number (e.g., '3.') and 'm' is a garbled '256'. This makes the text unintelligible.

comments.title: Grammar and capitalization in Introduction
comments.quote: This diversity of data points may result in a manifold with low density regions, which degrades the quality of the generated samples [11]. however, tends to deteriorate results [33, 36, 42] because the clusters may contain too few data points for the generator and discriminator to properly model their data distribution.
comments.explanation: The word 'however' is not capitalized, violating grammatical rules. Additionally, the sentence lacks a subject to perform the action 'tends to deteriorate', relying on an implied subject from the previous clause ('This diversity') which makes the transition jarring. This error is present in both the provided passage and the full paper context provided.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Objective Definition and Experimental Setup
comments.quote: Our objective is to model the underlying data distribution $p(\mathbf{x})$ as a mixture of conditional distributions $p(\mathbf{x} | \mathbf{h}_i)$ around each of $M$ instance feature vectors $\mathbf{h}_i$ in the dataset... Given the overlapping nature of these clusters... Note that when training IC-GAN we use all available training datapoints to condition the model.
comments.explanation: In Section 2, the authors define the objective function as a mixture over $M$ components, where $M$ is implicitly tied to the dataset size (introduced as $D = \{x_i\}_{i=1}^M$). However, in Section 3.1, the 'Unless stated otherwise' experimental setup specifies that they only 'store 1,000 training set instances' (effectively using 1,000 components). This creates a discrepancy: the theoretical definition implies a mixture over the entire training set, while the default experimental setup uses a sparse subset. This affects the reproducibility of the exact objective function discussed in Section 2 and the interpretation of the resulting density approximation.

comments.title: Underspecified Mechanism for Handling Multiple Neighbors
comments.quote: discriminator $D(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{h}_i)$ that discerns between real neighbors and generated neighbors of $\mathbf{h}_i$... Note that for each $\mathbf{h}_i$, real neighbors are sampled uniformly from $A_i$.
comments.explanation: The text describes sampling a set of real neighbors (plural) to evaluate the discriminator, yet the function signature is defined as $D(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{h}_i)$, suggesting a single input pair. While standard GANs often process these samples as a batch, the authors do not explicitly specify the aggregation mechanism (e.g., averaging feature representations, batch normalization across neighbors, or attention over the set). This detail is crucial for the training dynamics and reproducibility of the model.

comments.title: Training FID exception specificity
comments.quote: Our IC-GAN matches or improves upon the unconditional version of the same backbone architecture in terms of FID in all cases, except for training FID with the StyleGAN2 backbone at 256 x 256 resolution.
comments.explanation: The statement implies that for StyleGAN2 at 128 x 128, training FID was not a problem (since the exception is only mentioned for 256). However, Table 2 shows the Training FID for StyleGAN2 at 128 is 8.9 (IC-GAN) vs 8.8 (Uncond). While smaller than the gap at 256 (7.1 vs 9.6), IC-GAN still performs worse (higher FID). The text risks misguiding the reader about performance consistency across resolutions, as the statement implies success at the lower resolution where there was actually a failure, albeit a minor one.

comments.title: Incorrect number formatting
comments.quote: obtain a score of 37 . 2
comments.explanation: The number is displayed with an extraneous space between the integer and decimal parts. This is an OCR artifact that disrupts the readability of the mathematical value. While obvious to a reader to correct it to 37.2, it represents an error in the extracted text provided.

comments.title: OCR typo in bibliography entry
comments.quote: C. Bishop. _Pattern recognition and machine learning_. Spinger-Verlag, 2006.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'Spinger-Verlag' instead of the correct 'Springer-Verlag'. This is a clear OCR error given the standard citation for Christopher Bishop's seminal textbook and the high phonetic similarity. While not a technical flaw in the paper's methods, it is an error in the reference list that could mislead a reader attempting to find or verify the cited work.

comments.explanation: The subscript notation `_ch⇥_` is incomplete. It appears to be an OCR artifact of 'ch × [value]', likely from the surrounding context which contains 'ch × 64'. While the definition is clear, the notation is invalid as written.

comments.title: Grammar and capitalization in Introduction
comments.quote: This diversity of data points may result in a manifold with low density regions, which degrades the quality of the generated samples [11]. however, tends to deteriorate results [33, 36, 42] because the clusters may contain too few data points for the generator and discriminator to properly model their data distribution.
comments.explanation: The word 'however' is not capitalized, violating grammatical rules. Additionally, the sentence lacks a subject to perform the action 'tends to deteriorate', relying on an implied subject from the previous clause ('This diversity') which makes the transition jarring. This error is present in both the provided passage and the full paper context provided.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Objective Definition and Experimental Setup
comments.quote: Our objective is to model the underlying data distribution $p(\mathbf{x})$ as a mixture of conditional distributions $p(\mathbf{x} | \mathbf{h}_i)$ around each of $M$ instance feature vectors $\mathbf{h}_i$ in the dataset... Given the overlapping nature of these clusters... Note that when training IC-GAN we use all available training datapoints to condition the model.
comments.explanation: In Section 2, the authors define the objective function as a mixture over $M$ components, where $M$ is implicitly tied to the dataset size (introduced as $D = \{x_i\}_{i=1}^M$). However, in Section 3.1, the 'Unless stated otherwise' experimental setup specifies that they only 'store 1,000 training set instances' (effectively using 1,000 components). This creates a discrepancy: the theoretical definition implies a mixture over the entire training set, while the default experimental setup uses a sparse subset. This affects the reproducibility of the exact objective function discussed in Section 2 and the interpretation of the resulting density approximation.

comments.title: Underspecified Mechanism for Handling Multiple Neighbors
comments.quote: discriminator $D(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{h}_i)$ that discerns between real neighbors and generated neighbors of $\mathbf{h}_i$... Note that for each $\mathbf{h}_i$, real neighbors are sampled uniformly from $A_i$.
comments.explanation: The text describes sampling a set of real neighbors (plural) to evaluate the discriminator, yet the function signature is defined as $D(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{h}_i)$, suggesting a single input pair. While standard GANs often process these samples as a batch, the authors do not explicitly specify the aggregation mechanism (e.g., averaging feature representations, batch normalization across neighbors, or attention over the set). This detail is crucial for the training dynamics and reproducibility of the model.

comments.title: Training FID exception specificity
comments.quote: Our IC-GAN matches or improves upon the unconditional version of the same backbone architecture in terms of FID in all cases, except for training FID with the StyleGAN2 backbone at 256 x 256 resolution.
comments.explanation: The statement implies that for StyleGAN2 at 128 x 128, training FID was not a problem (since the exception is only mentioned for 256). However, Table 2 shows the Training FID for StyleGAN2 at 128 is 8.9 (IC-GAN) vs 8.8 (Uncond). While smaller than the gap at 256 (7.1 vs 9.6), IC-GAN still performs worse (higher FID). The text risks misguiding the reader about performance consistency across resolutions, as the statement implies success at the lower resolution where there was actually a failure, albeit a minor one.

comments.title: Incorrect number formatting
comments.quote: obtain a score of 37 . 2
comments.explanation: The number is displayed with an extraneous space between the integer and decimal parts. This is an OCR artifact that disrupts the readability of the mathematical value. While obvious to a reader to correct it to 37.2, it represents an error in the extracted text provided.

comments.title: Ambiguous Figure Layout Reference
comments.quote: For recall, we also observe improvements for large neighborhoods when storing few instances (left-hand side of right panel). Moreover, as expected, we notice that the value of k does not significantly affect precision (right panel).
comments.explanation: The text describes a two-panel figure (likely left: FID, right: Precision/Recall). While the description of Recall being in the 'left-hand side of right panel' implies the right panel is split (e.g., Left=Recall, Right=Precision), the reference to 'precision (right panel)' suggests the entire right panel might represent precision, or it misses the 'left-hand side' specification for Precision just as it did for Recall in the previous sentence. While not a technical error, this phrasing could confuse a reader expecting the standard FID convention where Precision is typically on the left and Recall on the right of the metrics section. It relies on the reader inferring the split layout implicitly from the previous clause.

comments.title: Sentence Fragments and Stylistic Informality
comments.quote: left-hand side of left panel
comments.explanation: The phrase 'left-hand side of left panel' is grammatically acceptable but reads as slightly redundant and informal ('of the' is missing). This is more a stylistic preference than a technical error, but it breaks the flow of the otherwise precise explanation of the figure's layout.

comments.title: OCR typo in bibliography entry
comments.quote: C. Bishop. _Pattern recognition and machine learning_. Spinger-Verlag, 2006.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'Spinger-Verlag' instead of the correct 'Springer-Verlag'. This is a clear OCR error given the standard citation for Christopher Bishop's seminal textbook and the high phonetic similarity. While not a technical flaw in the paper's methods, it is an error in the reference list that could mislead a reader attempting to find or verify the cited work.
